findings: The BWRX-300 is a 300 MWe water-cooled, natural circulation Small Modular Reactor (SMR) and is the tenth generation of the Boiling Water Reactor (BWR), evolved from the NRC-licensed 1,520 MWe ESBWR.

The BWRX-300 has a net electrical output of 300 MWe to the grid, a thermal output of 870 MWth, and in-house consumption of 10–30 MWe, using a direct-cycle BWR design with a Rankine thermodynamic cycle.
The BWRX-300 net capacity to the electric grid is 270–290 MWe (net to grid), with a single core thermal power of 870 MWth and in-house plant consumption of 10–30 MWe.

The BWRX-300 is a 300-megawatt electric light water reactor based on proven and licensed boiling water reactor (BWR) technology, with GE Hitachi aiming to reduce plant size by 90% compared to large-scale BWRs.

The BWRX-300 uses a natural circulation primary circuit (no forced circulation pumps), a direct thermodynamic cycle (Rankine), a dry underground containment vessel, and GNF2 fuel bundles in a 4-metre diameter reactor pressure vessel.

scenarios.name: Base Case
scenarios.description: AI demand grows strongly; grid and permitting constraints remain material but manageable with early planning and proactive interconnection agreements.
critical_uncertainties: pace of grid interconnection queue resolution

scenarios.name: Upside Case
scenarios.description: Grid interconnection, power procurement, and cooling supply chains improve faster than expected; capital remains abundant and permitting streamlines.

scenarios.name: Downside Case
scenarios.description: AI load growth outpaces grid expansion; permitting slows and power constraints become binding, compressing development timelines and stranding early investments.
critical_uncertainties: regulatory response to power demand growth
capital market appetite for constrained-location assets
